critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-constructed — temporal splitting, consistent preprocessing, matched input richness, and operationally relevant metrics are all correctly handled. I found a small number of genuine issues worth raising, none of which are severe enough individually to invalidate the experiment, but two of them are structurally important.

**Issue 1: Workflow/lifecycle signals conflate prediction time with observation time**

The specific claim: 'Case age, number of reassignments, and time since last analyst update' are included in both models as 'operationally available in the SOC queue while a case is open.'

Why this may be wrong: These signals are time-varying throughout the life of a case. Their values at the moment a model scores the case depend critically on *when* during the case lifecycle scoring occurs. If the training label is assigned at case closure but the feature value is sampled at some earlier moment, the feature values captured in training may not reflect the distribution seen at inference time. Worse, if feature values are captured at closure time (the easiest implementation choice), they include information about how the case resolved — e.g., cases that stayed open longer before resolution tend to be more complex and thus more likely to be confirmed malicious. This would constitute soft label leakage through the lifecycle features, not from the label column itself but correlated with it in a way not present at the actual scoring moment. The design says these are 'available while a case is open' but doesn't specify *when* they are snapshotted relative to the scoring decision. A misconfiguration here would produce plausible-looking AUPRC improvements that disappear in production where features are captured earlier in case lifetime.

What would constitute evidence: Define explicitly the point-in-time at which features are snapshotted (e.g., 'at the moment a new alert is added to the cluster' or 'at fixed 4-hour intervals'). Then verify that feature distributions for the training set are consistent with what will be available at inference time. An ablation removing lifecycle features and checking whether AUPRC drops more than expected would reveal whether they are quietly doing most of the work.

**Issue 2: Single-organization data creates a confounded comparison that may not generalize — and the hypothesis test cannot distinguish architecture benefit from data-regime benefit**

The specific claim: If the transformer's AUPRC exceeds the baseline's by ≥0.05 with p < 0.05, this establishes that 'the transformer architecture (or its capacity to model sequential patterns and fine-grained text) provides a genuine operational benefit.'

Why this may be wrong: Single-organization data means the alert vocabulary, severity distributions, IP space, and ticket writing style are all drawn from one environment. Transformer models are heavily pretrained on natural language, and much of their advantage on ticket text may come from semantic generalization that is useful specifically because this organization writes tickets in a style well-aligned with the pretraining corpus. A different organization with terser, more technical, or more abbreviated ticket text might show no advantage. The AUPRC gap is thus a measure of the transformer's advantage in *this data regime*, not in the architecture's capacity to model alert sequences generally. The hypothesis statement claims too much causal scope. This is not merely a generalization concern — the mechanism of failure is specific: if ticket text quality is what drives the AUPRC gap (which the ablation would reveal), then the conclusion 'sequential alert modeling provides value' is not supported even when the headline metric is positive.

What would constitute evidence: The ticket-only ablation (alert sequences removed from the transformer) and the alert-only ablation (ticket text removed) together would decompose the gain. If the transformer advantage collapses when ticket text is removed but persists without it, the conclusion shifts from 'sequential alert modeling' to 'better text encoding.' If the advantage is present in both ablations, the claim is better supported. The current design includes the alert-sequence-only ablation but frames it as a sensitivity check rather than a primary decomposition, which means the experiment could end with an ambiguous mechanism even if the headline hypothesis is confirmed.

**Issue 3: Statistical test on AUPRC requires care — bootstrap or permutation, not parametric**

The specific claim: 'Two-sided test, α = 0.05' will determine statistical significance of the AUPRC difference.

Why this may be wrong: AUPRC is a summary statistic of a curve, and its sampling distribution is non-normal, especially under class imbalance and with the temporal dependence structure of the test set (adjacent cases share infrastructure, campaigns, and analyst context). Standard parametric tests are not valid. If a bootstrap confidence interval is used, the bootstrap must respect temporal ordering — block bootstrap or time-series bootstrap — not i.i.d. resampling, which would underestimate variance by breaking temporal correlation structure.

What would constitute evidence: Specifying the exact test procedure (e.g., paired block bootstrap over time windows) and verifying that under a null permutation (randomly swapping model scores), the test rejects at the stated α rate. If i.i.d. bootstrap is used on a temporally correlated test set, the confidence intervals will be too narrow and the significance threshold too easy to cross.

**Summary:** The lifecycle feature snapshot ambiguity is the most dangerous issue because it could produce inflated results that disappear in deployment without any obvious signal. The mechanistic decomposition gap is the most important for the stated hypothesis, since a confirmed win on the headline metric could still leave the mechanism ambiguous. The statistical test issue is real but fixable by test choice. No issues were found with the temporal split design, the input-richness matching, the metric selection, or the label construction.
all_issues_raised: Lifecycle/workflow features (case age, reassignments, time since last update) are time-varying and their snapshot timing is unspecified, creating potential soft label leakage if captured at or near case closure rather than at the actual scoring moment.

The hypothesis test cannot distinguish architectural benefit (sequential alert modeling) from text-encoding benefit (pretrained LM on ticket text), because the ablation treating alert-only and ticket-only conditions is framed as a sensitivity check rather than a primary decomposition required to support the stated mechanism.

The statistical significance test on AUPRC requires a temporally-aware procedure (block bootstrap or equivalent); i.i.d. resampling underestimates variance due to temporal correlation in the test set, making the significance threshold artificially easy to cross.
all_issues_adjudicated: Lifecycle/workflow features (case age, reassignments, time since last update) are time-varying and their snapshot timing is unspecified, creating potential soft label leakage if captured at or near case closure rather than at the actual scoring moment.

The hypothesis test cannot distinguish architectural benefit (sequential alert modeling) from text-encoding benefit (pretrained LM on ticket text), because the ablation treating alert-only and ticket-only conditions is framed as a sensitivity check rather than a primary decomposition required to support the stated mechanism.

The statistical significance test on AUPRC requires a temporally-aware procedure (block bootstrap or equivalent); i.i.d. resampling underestimates variance due to temporal correlation in the test set, making the significance threshold artificially easy to cross.
